### Solution: Interfacing a 16×2 LCD with 8051 Microcontroller

This solution provides an 8051 assembly program to interface a 16×2 LCD display. The program reads a switch connected to P2.7: if the switch is high (1), it displays the string "HELLO" on line 1; if low (0), it displays the reversed string "OLLEH" on line 2. The string is stored in ROM starting at address 300H.

#### 1. Hardware Interfacing Diagram

Since an image cannot be generated, a detailed textual description of the connections is provided below.

> **Visual Representation: 8051 to 16×2 LCD Interface**
> 
> **Components**:
> - 8051 Microcontroller (e.g., AT89C51)
> - 16×2 LCD Module (e.g., HD44780-based)
> - Single-pole single-throw (SPST) switch connected to P2.7 with a pull-down resistor (10 kΩ to ground)
> 
> **Pin Connections**:
> 
> *Data Bus (8-bit mode)*:
> - LCD D0–D7 → 8051 Port 1 (P1.0–P1.7)
> 
> *Control Lines*:
> - LCD RS (Register Select) → 8051 P3.0
>   - RS = 0 for command, RS = 1 for data
> - LCD RW (Read/Write) → 8051 P3.1
>   - RW = 0 for write, RW = 1 for read
> - LCD EN (Enable) → 8051 P3.2
>   - High-to-low pulse to latch data/command
> 
> *Switch Interface*:
> - One terminal of switch → +5V (VCC)
> - Other terminal → 8051 P2.7 and also to pull-down resistor (10 kΩ to ground)
> 
> *Power*:
> - VCC and backlight LED anode → +5V
> - VSS, backlight LED cathode (if no internal resistor) → Ground
> - VEE (contrast) → Potentiometer wiper (e.g., 10 kΩ pot between VCC and ground)
> 
> **Layout Sketch**:

The LCD is placed to the right of the 8051, with data lines horizontally connected and control lines vertically aligned. The switch is placed above the 8051, connected to P2.7.

#### 2. Assembly Program

The program is structured into initialization, switch checking, and display routines. Key subroutines handle LCD commands, data writing, and delays. The string "HELLO" is stored in code memory at 300H.

;====================================================================
; 8051 LCD Interface Program
; Description: Displays "HELLO" on line 1 if P2.7=1, else displays
;              "OLLEH" on line 2 if P2.7=0.
; String stored at ROM address 300H.
; Assumptions: 8-bit LCD data mode, control pins as defined below.
;====================================================================

; Control Pin Definitions (using bit-addressable pins)
RS      BIT     P3.0        ; Register Select
RW      BIT     P3.1        ; Read/Write
EN      BIT     P3.2        ; Enable

; Switch Pin
SWITCH  BIT     P2.7        ; Switch input

; LCD Commands
LCD_CLR EQU     01H         ; Clear display
LCD_HOME EQU    02H         ; Return home
LCD_LN1  EQU    80H         ; DDRAM address for line 1 start
LCD_LN2  EQU    0C0H        ; DDRAM address for line 2 start
LCD_ON   EQU    0EH         ; Display on, cursor on
LCD_8BIT EQU    38H         ; 8-bit, 2 lines, 5x7 font

; Program and Data Memory Organization
        ORG     0000H       ; Reset vector
        LJMP    MAIN        ; Jump to main program

        ORG     0300H       ; String storage (as per question)
STRING: DB      'H','E','L','L','O'

        ORG     0050H       ; Main program start (after interrupt vectors)

;====================================================================
; MAIN PROGRAM
;====================================================================
MAIN:
        MOV     SP, #70H    ; Initialize stack pointer
        ACALL   LCD_INIT    ; Initialize LCD
        ACALL   CHECK_SW    ; Check switch and display accordingly
        SJMP    $           ; Infinite loop (halt)

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: CHECK_SW
; Description: Checks switch at P2.7 and calls appropriate display.
;====================================================================
CHECK_SW:
        JB      SWITCH, DISP_FWD   ; If switch=1, display forward
        ; else display reverse
        MOV     A, #LCD_LN2        ; Set cursor to line 2
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        ACALL   DISP_REV
        RET
DISP_FWD:
        MOV     A, #LCD_LN1        ; Set cursor to line 1
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        ACALL   DISP_FWD_STR
        RET

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: DISP_FWD_STR
; Description: Displays string from 300H in forward order (HELLO).
; Uses DPTR as pointer, loops 5 times (string length).
;====================================================================
DISP_FWD_STR:
        MOV     DPTR, #STRING      ; Point to string start
        MOV     R2, #05H           ; Counter = 5 characters
FWD_LOOP:
        CLR     A                  ; Clear A for MOVC
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR         ; Read character from code memory
        ACALL   LCD_DATA           ; Send to LCD
        INC     DPTR               ; Point to next character
        DJNZ    R2, FWD_LOOP       ; Decrement counter, loop if not zero
        RET

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: DISP_REV
; Description: Displays string from 300H in reverse order (OLLEH).
; DPTR starts at last character (STRING+4) and decrements.
;====================================================================
DISP_REV:
        MOV     DPTR, #STRING+4    ; Point to last character ('O')
        MOV     R2, #05H           ; Counter = 5 characters
REV_LOOP:
        CLR     A                  ; Clear A for MOVC
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR         ; Read character from code memory
        ACALL   LCD_DATA           ; Send to LCD
        DEC     DPTR               ; Point to previous character
        DJNZ    R2, REV_LOOP       ; Decrement counter, loop if not zero
        RET

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: LCD_INIT
; Description: Initializes LCD with 8-bit mode, 2 lines, 5x7 font,
; display on, clear display, and entry mode.
;====================================================================
LCD_INIT:
        ACALL   DELAY              ; Power-on delay
        MOV     A, #LCD_8BIT       ; Function set: 8-bit, 2 lines, 5x7
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        MOV     A, #LCD_ON         ; Display on, cursor on
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        MOV     A, #LCD_CLR        ; Clear display
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        MOV     A, #06H            ; Entry mode: increment, no shift
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        RET

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: LCD_CMD
; Description: Sends command in accumulator to LCD.
; Steps: RS=0 (command), RW=0 (write), send data, pulse EN high-low.
;====================================================================
LCD_CMD:
        CLR     RS                 ; RS=0 for command
        SJMP    LCD_WRITE          ; Jump to common write routine

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: LCD_DATA
; Description: Sends data in accumulator to LCD.
; Steps: RS=1 (data), RW=0 (write), send data, pulse EN high-low.
;====================================================================
LCD_DATA:
        SETB    RS                 ; RS=1 for data
        ; Fall through to LCD_WRITE

LCD_WRITE:
        CLR     RW                 ; RW=0 for write
        MOV     P1, A              ; Put data/command on Port 1
        SETB    EN                 ; EN=1
        ACALL   DELAY_SHORT        ; Pulse width > 450 ns
        CLR     EN                 ; EN=0 (high-to-low pulse latches data)
        ACALL   DELAY              ; Wait for LCD to process
        RET

;====================================================================
; DELAY SUBROUTINES
; Note: Actual delay values depend on crystal frequency.
; Assume 12 MHz clock (1 µs per machine cycle).
;====================================================================
DELAY:
        MOV     R3, #50            ; Outer loop count
DELAY_LOOP:
        MOV     R4, #255           ; Inner loop count
        DJNZ    R4, $              ; 2 cycles per iteration
        DJNZ    R3, DELAY_LOOP
        RET

DELAY_SHORT:                       ; Very short delay for EN pulse
        MOV     R5, #10
        DJNZ    R5, $
        RET

;====================================================================
        END

#### 3. Program Explanation

- **Initialization**: The LCD is initialized by sending a series of commands: function set (8-bit, 2 lines), display on, clear display, and entry mode set.
- **Switch Checking**: The program reads P2.7. If it is high (`JB SWITCH, DISP_FWD`), it sets the cursor to line 1 (address 80H) and calls `DISP_FWD_STR`. If low, it sets the cursor to line 2 (address C0H) and calls `DISP_REV`.
- **Forward Display**: `DISP_FWD_STR` uses DPTR to point to the string start (300H) and reads each character sequentially using `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`. It sends five characters to the LCD.
- **Reverse Display**: `DISP_REV` initializes DPTR to 304H (last character) and decrements DPTR after each character, sending the string in reverse order.
- **LCD Communication**: The subroutines `LCD_CMD` and `LCD_DATA` handle the control signals (RS, RW, EN) and data transfer via Port 1.
- **Delays**: `DELAY` provides sufficient wait time for LCD operations; `DELAY_SHORT` ensures the EN pulse width meets HD44780 timing requirements.

#### 4. Timing Considerations

For a 12 MHz 8051, each machine cycle is 1 µs. The `DELAY` subroutine provides approximately:
(50 \times 255 \times 2) + \text{overhead} \approx 25,500\ \mu\text{s} = 25.5\ \text{ms}

This meets the LCD power-on and command execution delays (typically >15 ms). The `DELAY_SHORT` provides about 20 µs, sufficient for the EN pulse width (>450 ns). Adjust delays if a different crystal is used.

#### 5. Alternative Approach (4-bit Mode)

For reduced I/O usage, the LCD can be interfaced in 4-bit mode (using only P1.4–P1.7). This would require modifying the initialization and data/command routines to send two nibbles per byte. However, the given problem does not specify the data mode, so the standard 8-bit mode is used for simplicity.

Switch State (P2.7) LCD Line Displayed String
High (1) Line 1 HELLO
Low (0) Line 2 OLLEH

Module 5: I/O interfacing with Microcont.visual_description: Markdown table describing LCD output based on switch state.

ORG 000BH
LJMP TIMER0_ISR

ORG 001BH
LJMP TIMER1_ISR

ORG 0030H
MAIN:
    MOV P1, #00H          ; Initialize count on Port P1 to 0
    MOV P2, #00H          ; Initialize display on Port P2 to 0
    MOV TMOD, #21H        ; Timer 0 in mode 1 (16-bit), Timer 1 in mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
    MOV TH0, #0FCH        ; Load Timer 0 for 1ms delay (assuming 12MHz crystal)
    MOV TL0, #018H
    MOV TH1, #0FCH        ; Load Timer 1 for 1 second delay (adjust as per crystal frequency)

    SETB ET1              ; Enable Timer 1 interrupt

TIMER0_ISR:

    MOV TH0, #0FCH        ; Reload Timer 0 for 1ms delay
    MOV TL0, #018H
    JB P3.4, NO_TOUCH     ; Check if sensor is touched (assuming active low)
    INC P1                ; Increment count on Port P1
NO_TOUCH:

TIMER1_ISR:
    CLR TF1               ; Clear Timer 1 overflow flag
    MOV P2, P1            ; Display touch count from Port P1 to Port P2
    RETI

### **Solution: 8051 Assembly Language Program for Capacitive Touch Sensor Interface**

#### **Objective**
Develop an assembly language program (ALP) for the 8051 microcontroller to:
1. Interface a capacitive touch sensor with pin **P3.4** (T0 input).
2. Use **Timer 0** as a counter to detect touch events.
3. On each touch, increment a count and display it immediately on **Port P1**.
4. Use **Timer 1** to generate a 1‑second periodic update, displaying the same count on **Port P2** every second.

#### **Assumptions**
- Crystal frequency: **12 MHz**, giving a timer increment period of **1 µs**.
- Capacitive touch sensor produces a clean pulse on P3.4 for each touch.
- Timer 0 is configured in **mode 2** (8‑bit auto‑reload counter) to count pulses.
- Timer 1 is configured in **mode 1** (16‑bit timer) to generate a **50 ms** interrupt; 20 such interrupts yield 1 second.
- `touch_count` is an 8‑bit variable (range 0–255) stored at RAM location `30H`.
- Ports P1 and P2 are configured as output ports.

---

#### **Algorithm**
1. **Initialization**
   - Set stack pointer (`SP`) to a safe location.
   - Clear `touch_count` and initialize a counter for 1‑second timing.
   - Configure **Timer 0** as a counter (mode 2) with reload value `0xFF` to overflow on each pulse.
   - Configure **Timer 1** as a timer (mode 1) with reload values for a 50 ms overflow.
   - Enable Timer 0 and Timer 1 interrupts, then start both timers.

2. **Interrupt Service Routines**
   - **Timer 0 ISR** (address `000BH`):
     - Increment `touch_count`.
     - Output updated `touch_count` to Port P1.
   - **Timer 1 ISR** (address `001BH`):
     - Reload Timer 1 for the next 50 ms.
     - Decrement the 1‑second counter; when it reaches zero:
       - Output `touch_count` to Port P2.
       - Reset the counter to 20.

3. **Main Loop**
   - Enter an infinite idle loop, waiting for interrupts.

---

#### **Assembly Code**

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; 8051 ALP: Capacitive Touch Sensor Interface
; Sensor connected to P3.4, Timer0 as counter, Timer1 for 1-sec updates
; Port P1 shows real-time count; Port P2 updates every second
; Crystal: 12 MHz -> timer increment = 1 µs
;------------------------------------------------------------------

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; Register and RAM definitions
;------------------------------------------------------------------
TOUCH_COUNT   EQU   30H        ; RAM location for touch count (8-bit)
OVERFLOW_CNT  EQU   31H        ; Counter for 1-sec timing (20 × 50 ms)

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; Reset and interrupt vectors
;------------------------------------------------------------------
        ORG     0000H          ; Reset vector
        LJMP    MAIN           ; Jump to main program

        ORG     000BH          ; Timer 0 interrupt vector
        LJMP    TIMER0_ISR     ; Jump to Timer 0 ISR

        ORG     001BH          ; Timer 1 interrupt vector
        LJMP    TIMER1_ISR     ; Jump to Timer 1 ISR

        ORG     0030H          ; Main program start address

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; MAIN: Initialization and infinite loop
;------------------------------------------------------------------
MAIN:
        ; Initialize stack pointer
        MOV     SP, #60H       ; Set SP to 60H

        ; Initialize variables
        MOV     TOUCH_COUNT, #00H   ; Clear touch count
        MOV     OVERFLOW_CNT, #14H  ; 20 decimal = 14H (for 1 sec)

        ; Initialize Ports (optional, clear outputs)
        MOV     P1, #00H
        MOV     P2, #00H

        ; Configure Timer 0 and Timer 1 modes
        ; TMOD: T1 mode 1 (16-bit timer), T0 mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload counter)
        ; T1: Gate=0, C/T=0 (timer), M1=0, M0=1 -> 0001 (high nibble)
        ; T0: Gate=0, C/T=1 (counter), M1=1, M0=0 -> 0110 (low nibble)
        ; TMOD = 0001 0110B = 16H
        MOV     TMOD, #16H

        ; Load Timer 0: mode 2, reload value = 0xFF
        MOV     TH0, #0FFH     ; Auto-reload value
        MOV     TL0, #0FFH     ; Initial count (first pulse causes overflow)

        ; Load Timer 1 for 50 ms (50000 counts @ 1 µs)
        ; Reload value = 65536 - 50000 = 15536 = 3CB0H
        MOV     TH1, #3CH      ; High byte = 3CH
        MOV     TL1, #0B0H     ; Low byte  = B0H

        ; Enable interrupts
        SETB    ET0            ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
        SETB    ET1            ; Enable Timer 1 interrupt
        SETB    EA             ; Enable global interrupts

        ; Start both timers
        SETB    TR0            ; Start Timer 0 (counter)
        SETB    TR1            ; Start Timer 1 (timer)

        ; Infinite idle loop
LOOP:
        SJMP    LOOP           ; Wait for interrupts

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; TIMER0_ISR: Counter interrupt for touch detection
;------------------------------------------------------------------
TIMER0_ISR:
        ; Interrupt flag TF0 is automatically cleared by hardware
        INC     TOUCH_COUNT    ; Increment touch count
        MOV     P1, TOUCH_COUNT ; Output to Port P1
        RETI                   ; Return from interrupt

;------------------------------------------------------------------
; TIMER1_ISR: Timer interrupt for 1-second periodic update
;------------------------------------------------------------------
TIMER1_ISR:
        ; Timer 1 mode 1 requires manual reload
        MOV     TH1, #3CH      ; Reload high byte for 50 ms
        MOV     TL1, #0B0H     ; Reload low byte

        ; Decrement 1-second counter
        DJNZ    OVERFLOW_CNT, EXIT_T1  ; Jump if not zero

        ; 1 second elapsed: output touch count to Port P2
        MOV     P2, TOUCH_COUNT
        MOV     OVERFLOW_CNT, #14H     ; Reset counter (20)

EXIT_T1:
        RETI                   ; Return from interrupt

;------------------------------------------------------------------
        END                    ; End of program

---

#### **Program Explanation**

1. **Timer 0 Configuration (Counter Mode)**
   - **TMOD** setting: `0x16` (binary `00010110`).
     - Timer 0: `C/T = 1` (counter), `M1M0 = 10` (mode 2, 8‑bit auto‑reload).
     - Timer 1: `C/T = 0` (timer), `M1M0 = 01` (mode 1, 16‑bit).
   - **TH0 = TL0 = 0xFF**: The counter starts at 255; the first pulse on P3.4 increments it to 0, causing an overflow and an interrupt. The auto‑reload feature immediately resets it to 0xFF for the next pulse.
   - Each pulse generates a Timer 0 interrupt, where `touch_count` is incremented and sent to Port P1.

2. **Timer 1 Configuration (1‑Second Timing)**
   - With a 12 MHz crystal, the timer increments every 1 µs.
   - To achieve a 1‑second period, we use a **50 ms** overflow and count 20 overflows.
   - Reload value for 50 ms:  
     $$ \text{Counts} = 50000 = 50000_{10} = \text{C350H} $$  
     $$ \text{Reload} = 65536 - 50000 = 15536_{10} = \text{3CB0H} $$
   - In the Timer 1 ISR, we reload TH1/TL1 with `3CB0H`, decrement `OVERFLOW_CNT`, and when it reaches zero, output `touch_count` to Port P2.

3. **Interrupt Handling**
   - **Timer 0 ISR**: Updates the touch count and Port P1 in real time.
   - **Timer 1 ISR**: Manages the 1‑second timing and updates Port P2 periodically.
   - Both interrupts are enabled, and the main program simply waits in an infinite loop.

4. **Memory Usage**
   - `TOUCH_COUNT` at `30H`: Stores the current touch count (0–255).
   - `OVERFLOW_CNT` at `31H`: Counts 50 ms overflows (initialized to 20).
   - Stack pointer set to `60H` to avoid conflict with these variables.

---

#### **Hardware Considerations**
- The capacitive touch sensor output should be connected to **P3.4** (T0 pin).
- Ports P1 and P2 are assumed to be connected to display units (e.g., LEDs or 7‑segment displays).
- If the sensor output is noisy or bounces, additional hardware or software debouncing may be required (not implemented here as per the problem statement).

---

#### **Visual Representation of System Architecture**

> **System Block Diagram**
> 
> * **Microcontroller**: 8051 core with internal timers and ports.
> * **Sensor Connection**: 
>   - Capacitive touch sensor output pin → **P3.4** (Timer 0 external input).
> * **Output Ports**:
>   - **Port P1**: Connected to 8 LEDs (real‑time touch count in binary).
>   - **Port P2**: Connected to an 8‑bit display (updated every second).
> * **Timers**:
>   - **Timer 0**: Configured as a **counter** (mode 2), counting pulses from P3.4.
>   - **Timer 1**: Configured as a **timer** (mode 1), generating 50 ms interrupts.
> * **Interrupt Flow**:
>   - Timer 0 overflow → ISR updates `touch_count` and Port P1.
>   - Timer 1 overflow → ISR reloads timer, counts 20 overflows, then updates Port P2.
> * **Data Paths**:
>   - Sensor pulse → Timer 0 counter → Interrupt → Increment `touch_count` → Port P1.
>   - Timer 1 interrupts → 1‑second elapsed → Read `touch_count` → Port P2.

This solution meets all specified requirements: Timer 0 detects touch events, Port P1 shows the updated count on each touch, and Timer 1 triggers a periodic display on Port P2 every second.

question.content: Develop an 8051 ALP to interface a 16x2 LCD display with a suitable diagram. Assume the string "VIT" is stored in ROM location from $200\text{H}$ onwards and the string "Vellore" is stored in ROM location from $300\text{H}$ onwards. A switch is connected to port pin $P0.4$. If the switch input is $1$, display the string "VIT" in line 1 of the LCD display; otherwise, display the string "Vellore" in line 2 of the LCD display.

ORG 0000H

; LCD Interface Pins (adjust based on your hardware)
RS EQU P2.0
RW EQU P2.1
EN EQU P2.2
DATA_PORT EQU P1
SWITCH EQU P0.4

; ROM Locations for Strings
VIT_STRING EQU 200H
VELLORE_STRING EQU 300H

MAIN:
    ACALL LCD_INIT
    ACALL DELAY

CHECK_SWITCH:
    JB SWITCH, DISPLAY_VIT
    ; Switch is 0, display Vellore on line 2
    MOV A, #0C0H          ; Command to move cursor to line 2
    ACALL LCD_COMMAND
    MOV DPTR, #VELLORE_STRING
    ACALL DISPLAY_STRING
    SJMP CHECK_SWITCH

DISPLAY_VIT:
    ; Switch is 1, display VIT on line 1
    MOV A, #80H           ; Command to move cursor to line 1
    ACALL LCD_COMMAND
    MOV DPTR, #VIT_STRING
    ACALL DISPLAY_STRING
    SJMP CHECK_SWITCH

; LCD Initialization Routine
LCD_INIT:
    MOV A, #38H           ; 8-bit, 2-line, 5x7 font
    ACALL LCD_COMMAND
    MOV A, #0CH           ; Display ON, cursor OFF
    ACALL LCD_COMMAND
    MOV A, #06H           ; Entry mode: increment cursor
    ACALL LCD_COMMAND
    MOV A, #01H           ; Clear display
    ACALL LCD_COMMAND
    RET

; Send command to LCD
LCD_COMMAND:
    MOV DATA_PORT, A
    CLR RS                ; RS=0 for command
    CLR RW                ; RW=0 for write
    SETB EN               ; Enable pulse
    ACALL DELAY
    CLR EN
    RET

; Send data to LCD
LCD_DATA:
    MOV DATA_PORT, A
    SETB RS               ; RS=1 for data
    CLR RW                ; RW=0 for write
    SETB EN               ; Enable pulse
    ACALL DELAY
    CLR EN
    RET

; Display string from ROM
DISPLAY_STRING:
    CLR A
    MOVC A, @A+DPTR       ; Get character from ROM
    JZ DISPLAY_END        ; If null terminator, end
    ACALL LCD_DATA
    INC DPTR
    SJMP DISPLAY_STRING
DISPLAY_END:
    RET

; Delay routine
DELAY:
    MOV R0, #50
DELAY_LOOP:
    MOV R1, #255
    DJNZ R1, $
    DJNZ R0, DELAY_LOOP
    RET

; Strings stored in ROM
ORG 200H
DB 'V', 'I', 'T', 0

ORG 300H
DB 'V', 'e', 'l', 'l', 'o', 'r', 'e', 0

END

answer.content:
### **Introduction**
The 8051 microcontroller interfaces with a 16×2 LCD display to show strings stored in ROM. A switch connected to P0.4 controls the display: if the switch input is high (logic 1), the string “VIT” is displayed on line 1; if low (logic 0), “Vellore” is displayed on line 2. The program continuously monitors the switch and updates the display accordingly.

### **Circuit Diagram Description**

> **Components:**
> - **8051 Microcontroller (e.g., AT89C51):** Provides control and data signals.
> - **16×2 LCD Module (HD44780 compatible):** Displays the strings.
> - **Switch:** Momentary push‑button connected between P0.4 and ground.
> - **Pull‑up Resistor:** 10 kΩ external resistor connected from P0.4 to Vcc (if internal pull‑up is insufficient).
> - **Power Supply:** +5 V DC for the 8051 and LCD.
>
> **Connections:**
> - **LCD Data Bus (8‑bit):** D0–D7 of LCD connected to Port 2 (P2.0–P2.7).
> - **LCD Control Pins:**
>   - RS (Register Select) → P3.0
>   - RW (Read/Write) → P3.1 (tied low for write‑only operation)
>   - E (Enable) → P3.2
> - **LCD Power:**
>   - VSS (Ground) → GND
>   - VDD (+5 V) → +5 V
>   - VEE (Contrast) → Wiper of a 10 kΩ potentiometer between +5 V and GND.
> - **Switch Connection:**
>   - One terminal → P0.4
>   - Other terminal → GND
>   - External pull‑up resistor from P0.4 to +5 V ensures a high level when the switch is open.
>
> **Clock:** 8051 crystal oscillator (typically 12 MHz) connected across XTAL1 and XTAL2.

### **Assumptions**
- The LCD operates in 8‑bit mode, with 2 lines and 5×7 character font.
- Strings are stored in code memory starting at addresses 200 H (“VIT”) and 300 H (“Vellore”), each null‑terminated.
- The switch is debounced (or the program includes a delay to avoid flickering).
- Port 0 requires an external pull‑up resistor for proper high logic level.

### **Program Explanation**
The program comprises:
1. **Initialization:** Sets the stack pointer, initializes the LCD (function set, display on, clear, entry mode).
2. **Switch Monitoring:** Reads P0.4; if high, displays “VIT” on line 1; if low, displays “Vellore” on line 2.
3. **LCD Routines:** Subroutines for sending commands and data, with appropriate delays.
4. **String Display:** Reads characters from ROM until a null terminator and sends them to the LCD.
5. **Main Loop:** Continuously checks the switch and updates the display.

**Pin Definitions:**
- Port 2: LCD data port.
- P3.0: RS (0 = command, 1 = data).
- P3.1: RW (0 = write, 1 = read; kept at 0).
- P3.2: E (enable pulse).
- P0.4: Switch input.

**LCD Commands Used:**
- `CLEAR` = `01H`
- `HOME` = `02H`
- `LINE1` = `80H` (DDRAM address for first line)
- `LINE2` = `C0H` (DDRAM address for second line)

;--------------------------------------------------------------------
; 8051 ALP to interface 16x2 LCD with switch on P0.4
; Strings: "VIT" at 200H, "Vellore" at 300H
; If P0.4 = 1, display "VIT" on line1; else "Vellore" on line2
;--------------------------------------------------------------------

; Pin definitions
RS      EQU     P3.0
RW      EQU     P3.1
E       EQU     P3.2
LCD_DATA EQU    P2
SWITCH  EQU     P0.4

; LCD command constants
CLEAR   EQU     01H
HOME    EQU     02H
LINE1   EQU     80H
LINE2   EQU     0C0H

        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

; String storage in ROM
        ORG     200H
STRING1: DB     'V','I','T',0        ; Null-terminated "VIT"

        ORG     300H
STRING2: DB     'V','e','l','l','o','r','e',0   ; Null-terminated "Vellore"

; Main program
        ORG     100H                 ; Avoid overlap with strings
MAIN:
        MOV     SP, #60H             ; Initialize stack pointer
        ACALL   LCD_INIT             ; Initialize LCD

CHECK_SWITCH:
        JB      SWITCH, DISPLAY_VIT  ; If switch high, display VIT
        ; Otherwise display Vellore on line 2
        MOV     DPTR, #STRING2       ; Pointer to "Vellore"
        MOV     A, #LINE2            ; Set cursor to line 2
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        ACALL   DISPLAY_STRING
        SJMP    WAIT_LOOP

DISPLAY_VIT:
        MOV     DPTR, #STRING1       ; Pointer to "VIT"
        MOV     A, #LINE1            ; Set cursor to line 1
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        ACALL   DISPLAY_STRING

WAIT_LOOP:
        ACALL   DELAY_1S             ; Wait before checking again
        SJMP    CHECK_SWITCH         ; Continuous monitoring

;--------------------------------------------------------------------
; LCD_INIT: Initialize LCD in 8-bit, 2-line, 5x7 mode
;--------------------------------------------------------------------
LCD_INIT:
        ACALL   DELAY_20MS           ; Power-on delay
        MOV     A, #38H              ; Function set: 8-bit, 2 lines, 5x7
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        MOV     A, #0CH              ; Display on, cursor off, blink off
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        MOV     A, #06H              ; Entry mode: increment, no shift
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        MOV     A, #CLEAR            ; Clear display
        ACALL   LCD_CMD
        RET

;--------------------------------------------------------------------
; LCD_CMD: Send command in accumulator to LCD
;--------------------------------------------------------------------
LCD_CMD:
        MOV     LCD_DATA, A          ; Output command to data port
        CLR     RS                   ; RS = 0 for command
        CLR     RW                   ; RW = 0 for write
        SETB    E                    ; Enable pulse high
        ACALL   DELAY_1MS            ; Pulse width
        CLR     E                    ; Enable low
        ACALL   DELAY_1MS            ; Command execution time
        RET

;--------------------------------------------------------------------
; LCD_DATA_WRITE: Send data in accumulator to LCD
;--------------------------------------------------------------------
LCD_DATA_WRITE:
        MOV     LCD_DATA, A          ; Output data to data port
        SETB    RS                   ; RS = 1 for data
        CLR     RW                   ; RW = 0 for write
        SETB    E                    ; Enable pulse high
        ACALL   DELAY_1MS            ; Pulse width
        CLR     E                    ; Enable low
        ACALL   DELAY_1MS            ; Data write time
        RET

;--------------------------------------------------------------------
; DISPLAY_STRING: Display null-terminated string pointed by DPTR
;--------------------------------------------------------------------
DISPLAY_STRING:
        PUSH    ACC                  ; Save accumulator
DS_LOOP:
        CLR     A                    ; Clear A for MOVC
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR           ; Get character from code memory
        JZ      DS_END               ; If null, end of string
        ACALL   LCD_DATA_WRITE       ; Display character
        INC     DPTR                 ; Point to next character
        SJMP    DS_LOOP
DS_END:
        POP     ACC                  ; Restore accumulator
        RET

;--------------------------------------------------------------------
; DELAY ROUTINES (for 12 MHz crystal)
;--------------------------------------------------------------------
DELAY_1MS:                           ; Approximately 1 ms delay
        MOV     R6, #2
LOOP1:  MOV     R7, #250
        DJNZ    R7, $
        DJNZ    R6, LOOP1
        RET

DELAY_20MS:                          ; Approximately 20 ms delay
        MOV     R5, #40
LOOP2:  ACALL   DELAY_1MS
        DJNZ    R5, LOOP2
        RET

DELAY_1S:                            ; Approximately 1 second delay
        MOV     R4, #200
LOOP3:  ACALL   DELAY_20MS
        DJNZ    R4, LOOP3
        RET

;--------------------------------------------------------------------
        END

### **Flowchart**

> **Program Flow:**
> 1. **Start**
> 2. **Initialize:**
>    - Set stack pointer
>    - Initialize LCD (function set, display on, clear display)
> 3. **Check Switch:**
>    - Read P0.4
>    - **If P0.4 = 1:**
>        - Set DDRAM address to line 1 (`80H`)
>        - Display string from 200 H (“VIT”)
>    - **Else (P0.4 = 0):**
>        - Set DDRAM address to line 2 (`C0H`)
>        - Display string from 300 H (“Vellore”)
> 4. **Delay** (1 s)
> 5. **Loop** back to step 3.
>
> **Subroutines:**
> - `LCD_CMD`: Send command in A to LCD.
> - `LCD_DATA_WRITE`: Send data in A to LCD.
> - `DISPLAY_STRING`: Display null‑terminated string pointed by DPTR.
> - Delay routines (`DELAY_1MS`, `DELAY_20MS`, `DELAY_1S`).

### **Note on Port 0 Configuration**
Port 0 of the 8051 is open‑drain and lacks internal pull‑up resistors. Therefore, an external 10 kΩ pull‑up resistor on P0.4 is necessary to ensure a logic high when the switch is open. Writing a ‘1’ to the port pin configures it as an input but does not provide a strong high; the external resistor is essential.

### **Conclusion**
The provided assembly program successfully interfaces the 8051 with a 16×2 LCD and a switch. The LCD displays “VIT” on the first line when the switch is high, and “Vellore” on the second line when the switch is low. The circuit diagram and code are detailed for easy implementation.

question.content: An 8051 microcontroller is connected to a linear temperature sensor through ADC0804. The sensor output voltage is linearly proportional to temperature, with $0\text{ V}$ at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $5\text{ V}$ at $100^{\circ}\text{C}$. At $100^{\circ}\text{C}$, the ADC0804 receives $5\text{ V}$. Develop an ALP with a suitable interfacing diagram to interface ADC0804 with 8051. Read the ADC value, store it in Accumulator A, and compare it with the threshold ADC value corresponding to $50^{\circ}\text{C}$ (assume an 8-bit ADC with $V_{\text{ref}} = 5\text{ V}$, so the threshold is $128$). If $A > \text{threshold}$, turn ON the buzzer connected to port P1.3 to alarm for $60\text{ ms}$ using timer 0.

answer.content:
### Interfacing Diagram

> **Visual Representation: ADC0804 Interfacing with 8051 Microcontroller**
> 
> **Components and Connections:**

1. **8051 Microcontroller** (40-pin DIP):
   - **Port 0 (P0.0–P0.7)**: Connected to ADC0804 data lines DB0–DB7 (pins 11–18).
   - **Port 1 (P1.3)**: Connected to the buzzer (via a current-limiting resistor and optional transistor driver).
   - **Port 2**:
       - **P2.0**: Connected to WR (pin 3) of ADC0804.
       - **P2.1**: Connected to RD (pin 2) of ADC0804.
       - **P2.2**: Connected to INTR (pin 5) of ADC0804.
   - **Power and Clock**:
       - **Vcc (pin 40)**: +5 V.
       - **GND (pin 20)**: Ground.
       - **EA (pin 31)**: Tied high (via pull-up resistor) for internal program memory.
       - **Crystal**: 12 MHz across pins 18 (XTAL2) and 19 (XTAL1) with two 22 pF capacitors to ground.

2. **ADC0804** (20-pin DIP):
   - **Pin 1 (CS)**: Connected to GND (always selected).
   - **Pin 2 (RD)**: Connected to P2.1 of 8051.
   - **Pin 3 (WR)**: Connected to P2.0 of 8051.
   - **Pin 4 (CLK IN) and Pin 19 (CLK R)**: Connected together via an RC network (10 kΩ resistor to Vcc and 150 pF capacitor to GND) to generate the internal clock (~640 kHz).
   - **Pin 5 (INTR)**: Connected to P2.2 of 8051.
   - **Pin 6 (Vin+)**: Connected to the temperature sensor output.
   - **Pin 7 (Vin-)**: Connected to GND.
   - **Pin 8 (AGND) and Pin 10 (DGND)**: Connected to common ground.
   - **Pin 9 (Vref/2)**: Connected to a 2.5 V reference (e.g., voltage divider with two 4.7 kΩ resistors from Vcc to GND, or a precision 2.5 V reference IC). This sets the full-scale input voltage to 5 V.
   - **Pins 11–18 (DB0–DB7)**: Connected to P0.0–P0.7 of 8051.
   - **Pin 20 (Vcc)**: Connected to +5 V.

3. **Temperature Sensor**:
   - Output (linear voltage: 0 V at 0°C, 5 V at 100°C) connected to ADC0804 Vin+ (pin 6).
   - Ground connected to common GND.
   - Supply voltage (if required) appropriately derived from +5 V.

4. **Buzzer**:
   - One terminal connected to P1.3 of 8051 through a 1 kΩ current-limiting resistor.
   - The other terminal connected to GND (assuming active-high buzzer). For higher current, an NPN transistor driver may be inserted between P1.3 and the buzzer.

**Layout Description**:
- The 8051 is placed centrally on the left, with the ADC0804 to its right.
- The temperature sensor is placed left of the ADC0804, connected via a short wire to Vin+.
- The buzzer is placed near the 8051, connected to Port 1.
- Data and control lines are shown as straight lines between the respective pins.
- Power rails (+5 V and GND) are shown as horizontal lines running across the top and bottom of the diagram, with connections to all components.
- The RC clock circuit is placed close to pins 4 and 19 of the ADC0804.
### Assembly Language Program (ALP)

; Program to interface ADC0804 with 8051, read temperature,
; compare with 50°C threshold (ADC value 128), and activate buzzer for 60 ms if temperature > 50°C.
; Assumptions:
; - Crystal frequency: 12 MHz => machine cycle = 1 µs.
; - ADC0804 connected as per interfacing diagram.
; - Control signals:
;     WR (start conversion) = P2.0
;     RD (read data) = P2.1
;     INTR (end of conversion) = P2.2 (active low)
; - Buzzer connected to P1.3 (active high).

; Register and bit definitions
WR      BIT     P2.0
RD      BIT     P2.1
INTR    BIT     P2.2
BUZZER  BIT     P1.3

; Main program
        ORG     0000H           ; Reset vector
        LJMP    MAIN            ; Jump to main program

        ORG     0030H           ; Main program start address
MAIN:
        ; Initialize stack pointer and I/O ports
        MOV     SP, #30H        ; Set stack pointer to 30H
        MOV     P0, #0FFH       ; Configure Port 0 as input (write 1s)

        ; Initialize control signals and buzzer
        SETB    WR              ; WR idle state high
        SETB    RD              ; RD idle state high
        CLR     BUZZER          ; Turn off buzzer initially

LOOP:
        ; Start conversion: give a low pulse on WR
        CLR     WR              ; WR = 0
        NOP                     ; Small delay for pulse width
        SETB    WR              ; Rising edge starts conversion

        ; Wait for conversion complete: poll INTR until it goes low
WAIT:
        JB      INTR, WAIT      ; Wait while INTR is high (conversion in progress)

        ; Conversion complete, read data
        CLR     RD              ; RD = 0 enables ADC output buffers
        NOP                     ; Small delay for data stability
        MOV     A, P0           ; Read ADC value from Port 0 into Accumulator
        SETB    RD              ; RD = 1 disables ADC output

        ; Compare with threshold (128)
        CJNE    A, #128, NOTEQUAL   ; Compare, jump if not equal
        ; If equal, A = 128 -> not greater, so no buzzer
        SJMP    NO_BUZZ

NOTEQUAL:
        JC      NO_BUZZ         ; If carry set, A < 128 -> no buzzer
        ; A > 128, so activate buzzer for 60 ms
        SETB    BUZZER          ; Turn on buzzer
        LCALL   DELAY_60MS      ; Call 60 ms delay
        CLR     BUZZER          ; Turn off buzzer after delay
        SJMP    CONTINUE

NO_BUZZ:
        CLR     BUZZER          ; Ensure buzzer is off

CONTINUE:
        ; Optional delay before next conversion (if needed)
        ; Here we simply loop back immediately
        SJMP    LOOP            ; Repeat continuously

; Subroutine: DELAY_60MS
; Generates a 60 ms delay using Timer 0 in mode 1.
; Assumes 12 MHz crystal, so timer increments every 1 µs.
; Required count for 60 ms = 60000. Preload value = 65536 - 60000 = 5536 = 0x15A0.
DELAY_60MS:
        MOV     TMOD, #01H      ; Timer 0, mode 1 (16-bit timer)
        MOV     TH0, #15H       ; High byte of 5536
        MOV     TL0, #0A0H      ; Low byte of 5536
        SETB    TR0             ; Start Timer 0
WAIT_T0:
        JNB     TF0, WAIT_T0    ; Wait for Timer 0 overflow flag
        CLR     TR0             ; Stop Timer 0
        CLR     TF0             ; Clear overflow flag
        RET

        END

**Program Explanation**:
- **Initialization**: Stack pointer and Port 0 are initialized. Control signals (WR, RD) are set high, and the buzzer is turned off.
- **Conversion Start**: A low pulse on WR initiates an ADC conversion.
- **Polling**: The INTR pin is monitored until it goes low, indicating conversion completion.
- **Data Read**: RD is pulled low to enable the ADC output buffers, and the digital value is read from Port 0 into the accumulator.
- **Threshold Comparison**: The value is compared with 128 (50°C). If greater, the buzzer is turned on, a 60 ms delay is generated using Timer 0, and then the buzzer is turned off. Otherwise, the buzzer remains off.
- **Loop**: The process repeats continuously for real-time monitoring.

**Timer Delay Calculation**:
With a 12 MHz crystal, the timer clock frequency is 1 MHz (period = 1 µs). For a 60 ms delay, the required number of counts is:
\[
\text{Counts} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 60000
\]
Using Timer 0 in 16-bit mode (max count = 65536), the preload value is:
\[
\text{Preload} = 65536 - 60000 = 5536 = 0x15A0
\]
Thus, TH0 is loaded with 0x15 and TL0 with 0xA0.

question.content: Assume that two sensors are connected to P3.4 and P3.5 of the 8051 Microcontroller as digital inputs, where a logic low pulse on P3.4 indicates a person entering and on P3.5 indicates a person exiting. The maximum capacity of the auditorium is $200$. Write an Assembly Language Program (ALP) to continuously monitor the sensors, maintain a count of people inside the auditorium, send the count to port P0, and glow an LED connected to P1.7 if the auditorium is full.

ORG 0000H

; Initialize variables
COUNT EQU 30H          ; Store count at internal RAM address 30H
MAX_CAPACITY EQU 200   ; Maximum capacity constant

; Initialize count to 0
MOV COUNT, #00H

; Main loop
MAIN:
    ; Check sensor P3.4 (person entering)
    JB P3.4, CHECK_EXIT  ; If P3.4 is high (no pulse), check exit sensor
    ; Debounce delay (optional, adjust as needed)
    CALL DELAY
    ; Check again to confirm low pulse
    JB P3.4, CHECK_EXIT
    ; Increment count
    MOV A, COUNT
    ADD A, #01H
    MOV COUNT, A
    ; Check if count exceeds max capacity
    MOV A, COUNT
    CJNE A, #MAX_CAPACITY+1, NOT_FULL_ENTER
    ; If count > 200, set to 200 (prevent overflow)
    MOV COUNT, #MAX_CAPACITY
NOT_FULL_ENTER:
    ; Update output
    CALL UPDATE_OUTPUT
    ; Wait for sensor to go high again
WAIT_HIGH_ENTER:
    JNB P3.4, WAIT_HIGH_ENTER
    CALL DELAY

CHECK_EXIT:
    ; Check sensor P3.5 (person exiting)
    JB P3.5, END_CHECK  ; If P3.5 is high (no pulse), end check
    ; Debounce delay
    CALL DELAY
    ; Check again to confirm low pulse
    JB P3.5, END_CHECK
    ; Decrement count
    MOV A, COUNT
    SUBB A, #01H
    MOV COUNT, A
    ; Check if count underflows (below 0)
    JNC NOT_UNDERFLOW
    ; If underflow, set count to 0
    MOV COUNT, #00H
NOT_UNDERFLOW:
    ; Update output
    CALL UPDATE_OUTPUT
    ; Wait for sensor to go high again
WAIT_HIGH_EXIT:
    JNB P3.5, WAIT_HIGH_EXIT
    CALL DELAY

END_CHECK:
    ; Send count to port P0
    MOV P0, COUNT
    ; Check if auditorium is full
    MOV A, COUNT
    CJNE A, #MAX_CAPACITY, NOT_FULL
    ; If full (count = 200), glow LED at P1.7 (set to 1)
    SETB P1.7
    SJMP MAIN
NOT_FULL:
    ; If not full, turn off LED (set to 0)
    CLR P1.7
    SJMP MAIN

; Subroutine to update output (send count to P0 and check LED)
UPDATE_OUTPUT:
    MOV P0, COUNT
    MOV A, COUNT
    CJNE A, #MAX_CAPACITY, UPDATE_NOT_FULL
    SETB P1.7
    RET
UPDATE_NOT_FULL:
    CLR P1.7
    RET

; Delay subroutine for debouncing (adjust time as needed)
DELAY:
    MOV R2, #0FFH
DELAY_LOOP:
    DJNZ R2, DELAY_LOOP
    RET

END

answer.content:
### Solution

We design an **8051 Assembly Language Program (ALP)** to monitor two digital sensors (P3.4 for entry, P3.5 for exit), maintain a count of occupants (0–200), output the count to Port 0, and illuminate an LED on P1.7 when the auditorium is full (count = 200). The sensors generate **active-low pulses** (logic 0), so edge detection and debouncing are critical for accurate counting.

---

### **Algorithm Overview**
1. **Initialize**:
   - Set the count to 0.
   - Configure P3.4 and P3.5 as inputs (write `1` to these pins).
   - Configure P0 (8-bit output) and P1.7 (LED output).
   - Initialize edge-detection flags for both sensors.

2. **Main Loop**:
   - Read the current state of P3.4 and P3.5.
   - Detect **falling edges** (1 → 0 transition) for each sensor.
   - On a valid entry edge, increment count if `< 200`.
   - On a valid exit edge, decrement count if `> 0`.
   - Output the count to P0.
   - If count = 200, set P1.7 (LED ON); else clear P1.7 (LED OFF).
   - Add a debouncing delay to avoid multiple counts per pulse.

3. **Edge Detection**:
   - Use **previous-state storage** (in bit-addressable RAM) to identify transitions.
   - Reset detection only after observing a subsequent rising edge (0 → 1) to avoid re-triggering on the same pulse.

---

;*****************************************************************
; 8051 ALP for Auditorium Occupancy Monitoring
; Sensors: P3.4 (Entry), P3.5 (Exit) - Active Low Pulse
; Output: P0 (8-bit count), P1.7 (LED ON when count = 200)
; Maximum Capacity: 200 (fits in 8 bits: 200 ≤ 255)
;*****************************************************************

; --- Bit Definitions for Edge Detection (Bit-Addressable RAM 20H-2FH) ---
LAST_ENTRY   BIT   00H      ; Bit 0 of 20H: Last state of Entry sensor (P3.4)
LAST_EXIT    BIT   01H      ; Bit 1 of 20H: Last state of Exit sensor (P3.5)

; --- Register Usage ---
COUNT        EQU   R2       ; 8-bit occupancy count (0-200)
TEMP         EQU   R3       ; Temporary storage for port reads

; --- Program Start ---
        ORG   0000H         ; Reset vector
        LJMP  MAIN
        ORG   0030H         ; Code start after interrupt vectors

MAIN:
        MOV   SP, #60H      ; Initialize stack pointer

        ; Initialize variables
        MOV   COUNT, #0     ; Start with empty auditorium
        SETB  LAST_ENTRY    ; Assume sensors idle at HIGH
        SETB  LAST_EXIT

        ; Configure ports
        MOV   P0, #0        ; Clear P0 (count display)
        CLR   P1.7          ; LED OFF initially
        ; P3.4 and P3.5 are inputs; writing 1 configures as input
        SETB  P3.4
        SETB  P3.5

POLL_LOOP:
        ; --- Check ENTRY sensor (P3.4) for falling edge ---
        MOV   C, LAST_ENTRY ; Load previous state of Entry sensor
        ANL   C, /P3.4      ; C = LAST_ENTRY AND (NOT current state)
        JC    ENTRY_FALLING ; If C=1 → falling edge (LAST=1, CURRENT=0)
        JMP   CHECK_EXIT    ; Else, check exit sensor

ENTRY_FALLING:
        ; Valid falling edge on Entry sensor
        MOV   A, COUNT
        CJNE  A, #200, NOT_FULL_ENTRY  ; Compare count with 200
        JMP   ENTRY_RISING_WAIT        ; If count = 200, ignore increment

NOT_FULL_ENTRY:
        JC    DO_INCREMENT   ; If count < 200, increment
        JMP   ENTRY_RISING_WAIT

DO_INCREMENT:
        INC   COUNT          ; Increment occupancy
        CLR   LAST_ENTRY     ; Set last state = 0 (wait for rising edge)

ENTRY_RISING_WAIT:
        ; Wait for rising edge before allowing next detection
        MOV   C, P3.4
        JC    ENTRY_RISED    ; If P3.4 = 1, rising edge detected
        JMP   CHECK_EXIT     ; Still low, continue

ENTRY_RISED:
        SETB  LAST_ENTRY     ; Update last state to 1 (ready for next falling edge)

        ; --- Check EXIT sensor (P3.5) for falling edge ---
CHECK_EXIT:
        MOV   C, LAST_EXIT   ; Load previous state of Exit sensor
        ANL   C, /P3.5       ; C = LAST_EXIT AND (NOT current state)
        JC    EXIT_FALLING   ; Falling edge detected
        JMP   UPDATE_OUTPUT

EXIT_FALLING:
        ; Valid falling edge on Exit sensor
        MOV   A, COUNT
        JZ    EXIT_RISING_WAIT ; If count = 0, ignore decrement
        DEC   COUNT          ; Decrement occupancy
        CLR   LAST_EXIT      ; Set last state = 0 (wait for rising edge)

EXIT_RISING_WAIT:
        MOV   C, P3.5
        JC    EXIT_RISED     ; Rising edge detected
        JMP   UPDATE_OUTPUT

EXIT_RISED:
        SETB  LAST_EXIT      ; Update last state to 1

        ; --- Update outputs ---
UPDATE_OUTPUT:
        MOV   P0, COUNT      ; Send current count to Port 0

        ; Check if full (count = 200)
        MOV   A, COUNT
        CJNE  A, #200, NOT_FULL
        SETB  P1.7           ; LED ON (auditorium full)
        JMP   DELAY_LOOP

NOT_FULL:
        CLR   P1.7           ; LED OFF

        ; --- Debouncing delay (adjust for pulse width) ---
DELAY_LOOP:
        MOV   R5, #10        ; Outer loop counter
DELAY_OUTER:
        MOV   R6, #200       ; Middle loop
DELAY_MIDDLE:
        MOV   R7, #250       ; Inner loop
DELAY_INNER:
        DJNZ  R7, DELAY_INNER
        DJNZ  R6, DELAY_MIDDLE
        DJNZ  R5, DELAY_OUTER

        SJMP  POLL_LOOP      ; Repeat indefinitely

        END

---

### **Code Explanation**

#### **1. Initialization**
- `COUNT` (R2) stores the current occupancy (8-bit, range 0–200).
- `LAST_ENTRY` and `LAST_EXIT` bits store the previous logic levels of P3.4 and P3.5 for edge detection.
- Ports are configured:
  - **P3.4/P3.5**: Written with `1` to set as inputs.
  - **P0**: Output for count; cleared initially.
  - **P1.7**: Output for LED; cleared initially.

#### **2. Edge Detection Logic**
- **Entry (P3.4)**:
  - A **falling edge** is detected when `LAST_ENTRY = 1` and `P3.4 = 0`.
  - If `COUNT < 200`, increment `COUNT`.
  - Set `LAST_ENTRY = 0` until a **rising edge** (`P3.4 = 1`) resets it to `1`.
- **Exit (P3.5)**:
  - Similar detection: `LAST_EXIT = 1` and `P3.5 = 0` → falling edge.
  - If `COUNT > 0`, decrement `COUNT`.
  - Rising edge resets `LAST_EXIT` to `1`.

This **stateful detection** ensures each pulse is counted **once**, even if the sensor remains low for an extended period.

#### **3. Output Update**
- **P0**: Always outputs the 8-bit `COUNT`. Since 200 ≤ 255, no overflow occurs.
- **P1.7**: Set (logic 1) only when `COUNT = 200`, turning the LED ON. Otherwise, cleared (logic 0).

#### **4. Debouncing Delay**
- A nested loop delay (~10 ms with 12 MHz crystal) prevents multiple counts due to contact bounce.
- Adjust loop constants (`R5`, `R6`, `R7`) based on the actual clock frequency and sensor characteristics.

---

Resource Purpose
R2 Occupancy count (0–200)
R3 Temporary storage for P3 read
R5–R7 Delay loop counters
Bit 00H LAST_ENTRY flag (20H.0)
Bit 01H LAST_EXIT flag (20H.1)
P0 Output port for count
P1.7 LED output (1 = ON)
P3.4 Entry sensor input (active low)
P3.5 Exit sensor input (active low)

---

### **Visual Representation of System Architecture**

> **Diagram Description: 8051-Based Occupancy Monitoring System**
>
> * **8051 Microcontroller**: Central unit with internal RAM, registers, and ports.
> * **Input Sensors**:
>   - *Entry Sensor*: Connected to **P3.4**. Normally high (1), pulls low (0) when a person enters.
>   - *Exit Sensor*: Connected to **P3.5**. Normally high (1), pulls low (0) when a person exits.
> * **Output Devices**:
>   - *8-bit Display/Port*: **P0** outputs binary count (e.g., to LEDs or an external decoder).
>   - *Full Indicator*: **LED** at **P1.7** glows when count = 200.
> * **Data Flow**:
>   1. Sensors generate active-low pulses.
>   2. 8051 polls P3.4/P3.5, detects edges, updates count in `R2`.
>   3. Count sent to P0 continuously.
>   4. P1.7 set/clear based on count comparison.
> * **Debouncing**: Software delay routine filters mechanical switch bounce.

---

### **Mathematical Justification**
- **Maximum Count**: $200_{10} = 11001000_2$ → fits in 8 bits.
- **Edge Detection**:
  - Let $S_{\text{prev}}$ be the previous state and $S_{\text{curr}}$ the current state.
  - Falling edge condition: $S_{\text{prev}} = 1 \land S_{\text{curr}} = 0$.
  - In assembly: `MOV C, LAST_ENTRY` followed by `ANL C, /P3.4` computes $C = S_{\text{prev}} \cdot \overline{S_{\text{curr}}}$.
t_d \approx \frac{R5 \times R6 \times R7 \times 2}{\text{frequency}} = \frac{10 \times 200 \times 250 \times 2}{12 \times 10^6} \approx 8.33 \text{ ms}

---

### **Key Considerations**
1. **Pulse Width**: Sensors must generate pulses longer than the debouncing delay to avoid missed counts.
2. **Polling Rate**: The main loop execution time (including delay) determines maximum detectable pulse frequency.
3. **Count Accuracy**: Edge detection with state flags ensures no double-counting, even for long-duration low signals.
4. **Expandability**: For counts >255, use 16-bit arithmetic (e.g., `R2:R3`) and modify output logic accordingly.

This solution provides a robust, real-time occupancy monitoring system using 8051 assembly, with clear edge detection, debouncing, and output control.

question.content: Interface the gas sensor with an 8051 microcontroller using the ADC0804 ADC. Write an Assembly Language Program (ALP) outline to read the analog voltage from the sensor via the ADC0804 and implement a response (e.g., control an LED or buzzer) based on predefined gas concentration thresholds. Specify the use of 8051 I/O ports for the interface and the method for initiating conversion and reading the digital output.

ORG 0000H

; Define I/O Ports
ADC_DATA_PORT EQU P1    ; 8-bit data from ADC0804 (connected to P1)
ADC_CONTROL_PORT EQU P2 ; Control signals (connected to P2)
LED_PORT EQU P3         ; For LED/buzzer output (connected to P3)

; Define Control Pins (using bits of P2)
ADC_CS BIT P2.0         ; Chip Select (active low)
ADC_RD BIT P2.1         ; Read (active low)
ADC_WR BIT P2.2         ; Write (active low)
ADC_INTR BIT P2.3       ; Interrupt (active low, indicates conversion complete)

; Define Thresholds (example values, adjust based on sensor calibration)
THRESHOLD_LOW EQU 50    ; Lower threshold for gas concentration
THRESHOLD_HIGH EQU 150  ; Higher threshold for gas concentration

; Main Program
MAIN:
    MOV SP, #30H        ; Initialize stack pointer
    CLR LED_PORT        ; Turn off LED/buzzer initially

READ_ADC:
    ; Step 1: Initiate conversion
    CLR ADC_CS          ; Select ADC chip
    CLR ADC_WR          ; Start conversion (low pulse on WR)
    SETB ADC_WR         ; End pulse
    SETB ADC_CS         ; Deselect ADC

    ; Step 2: Wait for conversion to complete
WAIT_CONVERSION:
    JB ADC_INTR, WAIT_CONVERSION ; Poll INTR pin (high means conversion in progress)

    ; Step 3: Read digital output
    CLR ADC_CS          ; Select ADC chip
    CLR ADC_RD          ; Enable read (low pulse on RD)
    MOV A, ADC_DATA_PORT ; Read 8-bit data from ADC into accumulator
    SETB ADC_RD         ; End read
    SETB ADC_CS         ; Deselect ADC

    ; Step 4: Process data and implement response
    ; Compare with thresholds
    CJNE A, #THRESHOLD_LOW, CHECK_HIGH
    ; If A < THRESHOLD_LOW (gas concentration low)
    CLR LED_PORT.0      ; Turn off LED/buzzer (example: clear bit 0 of P3)
    SJMP READ_ADC       ; Loop back to read again

CHECK_HIGH:
    JC BELOW_LOW        ; If A < THRESHOLD_LOW, jump to BELOW_LOW
    CJNE A, #THRESHOLD_HIGH, CHECK_MID
    ; If A >= THRESHOLD_HIGH (gas concentration high)
    SETB LED_PORT.0     ; Turn on LED/buzzer (example: set bit 0 of P3)
    SJMP READ_ADC

CHECK_MID:
    JC BELOW_HIGH       ; If A < THRESHOLD_HIGH, jump to BELOW_HIGH
    ; If A >= THRESHOLD_HIGH (already handled above)
    SJMP READ_ADC

BELOW_LOW:
    CLR LED_PORT.0      ; Ensure LED/buzzer off for low concentration
    SJMP READ_ADC

BELOW_HIGH:
    ; For mid-range concentration (between low and high thresholds)
    ; Example: Blink LED or activate buzzer intermittently
    SETB LED_PORT.0     ; Turn on
    ACALL DELAY         ; Call delay subroutine
    CLR LED_PORT.0      ; Turn off
    ACALL DELAY         ; Call delay subroutine
    SJMP READ_ADC

; Delay Subroutine (example, adjust for timing needs)
DELAY:
    MOV R2, #0FFH
DELAY_LOOP:
    DJNZ R2, DELAY_LOOP
    RET

END

**Explanation of the ALP Outline:**

1. **I/O Port Interface:**
   - **ADC0804 Connection:**
     - Data pins (D0-D7) connected to Port 1 (P1) of the 8051 for reading the 8-bit digital output.
     - Control pins (CS, RD, WR, INTR) connected to Port 2 (P2) for managing ADC operations.
   - **Response Output:** Port 3 (P3) used to control an LED or buzzer (e.g., bit 0 for on/off).

2. **Method for Initiating Conversion and Reading Output:**
   - **Initiate Conversion:** Pulse the WR pin low while CS is low to start the analog-to-digital conversion.
   - **Wait for Completion:** Poll the INTR pin; it goes low when conversion is complete.
   - **Read Digital Output:** Pulse the RD pin low while CS is low to read the 8-bit data from the ADC into the accumulator.

3. **Response Based on Thresholds:**
   - Predefined thresholds (e.g., THRESHOLD_LOW = 50, THRESHOLD_HIGH = 150) are compared with the ADC value.
   - **Low Concentration (< THRESHOLD_LOW):** Turn off the LED/buzzer.
   - **Mid Concentration (between thresholds):** Implement an intermittent response (e.g., blinking LED).
   - **High Concentration (>= THRESHOLD_HIGH):** Turn on the LED/buzzer continuously.

4. **Additional Notes:**
   - The program uses polling for the INTR pin; interrupts could be used as an alternative.
   - Delay subroutine is included for timing in the mid-range response; adjust values based on clock frequency.
   - Ensure proper hardware connections (e.g., Vref/2 for ADC0804 reference voltage) and initialization in the actual setup.

answer.content:
### Introduction

In embedded systems, gas sensors are critical for safety and environmental monitoring. These sensors produce an analog voltage proportional to gas concentration, which must be converted to digital form for processing by a microcontroller. The 8051 microcontroller lacks an internal analog-to-digital converter (ADC), necessitating an external ADC such as the ADC0804. This solution details the hardware interface between a gas sensor, the ADC0804, and the 8051, along with an Assembly Language Program (ALP) outline to read the sensor data and trigger responses based on predefined concentration thresholds.

### Hardware Interface

The ADC0804 is an 8-bit successive approximation ADC with a resolution of 1 LSB = $V_{\text{ref}}/256$. It interfaces with the 8051 via parallel I/O ports for data transfer and control signals. The gas sensor’s output (analog voltage) is connected to the ADC’s analog input pin (Vin+). The digital outputs (D0–D7) of the ADC are connected to Port 1 of the 8051, configured as an input port. Control signals are managed via Port 2 pins as follows:

- **Chip Select (CS)**: Active low, connected to P2.0. When low, the ADC is selected for communication.
- **Write (WR)**: Active low, connected to P2.1. A low-to-high pulse on this pin initiates conversion.
- **Read (RD)**: Active low, connected to P2.2. When low, the ADC places converted data on its output lines.
- **Interrupt (INTR)**: Active low, connected to P2.3. Goes low when conversion is complete; can be polled to determine status.

Power supply and reference voltage connections for the ADC0804 are assumed standard (Vcc = +5 V, Vref/2 left open for 2.5 V reference, giving a 0–5 V input range). A clock circuit is provided via an RC network on CLK R and CLK IN pins.

### Software Algorithm

The ALP follows these steps:

1. **Initialize**: Configure Port 1 as input (if necessary; by default, ports are output) and Port 0 as output for LEDs and buzzer. Set initial states (all outputs off).
2. **Start Conversion**: Select the ADC by setting CS low, then apply a low-to-high pulse on WR to initiate conversion.
3. **Wait for Conversion**: Poll the INTR pin until it goes low, indicating conversion completion.
4. **Read Data**: Pulse RD low to enable the ADC’s output buffers, then read the digital value from Port 1.
5. **Process Data**: Compare the digital value with predefined thresholds to determine gas concentration level.
6. **Actuate Response**: Based on the level, turn on appropriate LEDs or buzzer.
7. **Repeat**: Loop continuously for continuous monitoring.

Thresholds are defined as constants:
- **TH_LOW**: Upper limit for safe zone (e.g., 80 decimal, corresponding to ~1.56 V).
- **TH_HIGH**: Upper limit for warning zone (e.g., 160 decimal, corresponding to ~3.13 V). Values above this indicate danger.

### Assembly Language Program Outline

The following ALP outline is written for the 8051 using standard assembly directives. Constants and port assignments are defined at the beginning. The code is structured as an infinite loop with subroutine calls for ADC read and response control.

;=====================================================================
; 8051 ALP for Gas Sensor Interface with ADC0804
; Assumptions: 
;   - ADC0804 data bus connected to Port 1
;   - Control signals: CS on P2.0, WR on P2.1, RD on P2.2, INTR on P2.3
;   - Output devices on Port 0: P0.0=Green LED, P0.1=Yellow LED, 
;                               P0.2=Red LED, P0.3=Buzzer
;=====================================================================

; Port and Pin Definitions
ADC_DATA   EQU   P1          ; Port for ADC data input
CS         EQU   P2.0        ; Chip Select (active low)
WR         EQU   P2.1        ; Write (active low)
RD         EQU   P2.2        ; Read (active low)
INTR       EQU   P2.3        ; Interrupt (active low)
OUT_PORT   EQU   P0          ; Output port for LEDs and buzzer

; Threshold Constants (8-bit, adjust based on sensor calibration)
TH_LOW     EQU   80          ; Decimal, safe zone if ADC value < TH_LOW
TH_HIGH    EQU   160         ; Decimal, warning if ADC value between TH_LOW and TH_HIGH

; Register Definitions
VAL        EQU   30H         ; RAM location to store ADC value

;---------------------------------------------------------------------
; Main Program
;---------------------------------------------------------------------
        ORG   0000H          ; Reset vector
        LJMP  MAIN

        ORG   0030H          ; Start of code
MAIN:   MOV   OUT_PORT, #00H ; Turn off all outputs initially
        MOV   A, #0FFH
        MOV   ADC_DATA, A    ; Configure Port 1 as input (write 1s)

LOOP:   ACALL READ_ADC       ; Read ADC value into VAL
        ACALL PROCESS        ; Compare and control outputs
        SJMP  LOOP           ; Infinite loop

;---------------------------------------------------------------------
; Subroutine: READ_ADC
; Purpose: Read 8-bit digital value from ADC0804 and store in VAL.
;---------------------------------------------------------------------
READ_ADC:
        CLR   CS             ; Select ADC (CS active low)
        CLR   WR             ; Start conversion: WR low
        NOP                  ; Short delay for signal stability
        SETB  WR             ; WR high -> low-to-high pulse
        SETB  CS             ; Deselect ADC (optional during conversion)

WAIT:   JB    INTR, WAIT     ; Poll INTR until it goes low (conversion done)

        CLR   CS             ; Select ADC again for reading
        CLR   RD             ; RD low to enable output buffers
        NOP                  ; Short delay for data stability
        MOV   A, ADC_DATA    ; Read data from Port 1
        MOV   VAL, A         ; Store in RAM
        SETB  RD             ; Disable output buffers
        SETB  CS             ; Deselect ADC
        RET

;---------------------------------------------------------------------
; Subroutine: PROCESS
; Purpose: Compare VAL with thresholds and activate outputs accordingly.
;---------------------------------------------------------------------
PROCESS:
        MOV   A, VAL         ; Load ADC value
        ; Check if value < TH_LOW (Safe zone)
        CJNE  A, #TH_LOW, CHECK_LOW
CHECK_LOW:
        JNC   CHECK_MID      ; If A >= TH_LOW, jump to next check
        ; Safe: Green LED on, others off
        MOV   OUT_PORT, #01H ; P0.0 = 1 (Green LED)
        RET

CHECK_MID:
        ; Check if value < TH_HIGH (Warning zone)
        CJNE  A, #TH_HIGH, CHECK_HIGH
CHECK_HIGH:
        JNC   DANGER         ; If A >= TH_HIGH, jump to danger
        ; Warning: Yellow LED on, others off
        MOV   OUT_PORT, #02H ; P0.1 = 1 (Yellow LED)
        RET

DANGER: ; Danger: Red LED and buzzer on
        MOV   OUT_PORT, #0CH ; P0.2 = 1 (Red LED), P0.3 = 1 (Buzzer)
        RET

;---------------------------------------------------------------------
        END

### Explanation of Key Parts

#### Conversion Initiation
The ADC0804 conversion is started by a low-to-high transition on the WR pin while CS is low. The sequence:
1. Set CS low to select the ADC.
2. Set WR low, then high, generating a rising edge that triggers conversion.

#### Reading Digital Output
After conversion, the INTR pin goes low. The program polls this pin until it detects a low state. Then, with CS low, RD is pulsed low to enable the ADC’s tri-state output buffers, and the data is read from Port 1.

#### Threshold Implementation
Thresholds are implemented using compare and jump instructions. The `CJNE` (Compare and Jump if Not Equal) instruction sets the carry flag based on the comparison between the accumulator and the immediate threshold value. Follow-up conditional jumps (`JNC` or `JC`) branch to appropriate code segments to activate outputs.

#### Response Control
Outputs are controlled by writing specific bit patterns to Port 0:
- Safe zone (`VAL < TH_LOW`): Green LED (P0.0) on.
- Warning zone (`TH_LOW ≤ VAL < TH_HIGH`): Yellow LED (P0.1) on.
- Danger zone (`VAL ≥ TH_HIGH`): Red LED (P0.2) and buzzer (P0.3) on.

All other bits of Port 0 remain low to ensure only the intended devices are activated.

### Conclusion

This solution provides a complete interface between a gas sensor and an 8051 microcontroller using the ADC0804 ADC. The hardware connections are explicitly defined, and the ALP outline demonstrates the steps to read analog data, compare it with thresholds, and control output devices. The code uses polling for simplicity but can be adapted to use interrupts for efficient operation. This framework can be extended with additional features like serial communication or display integration for real-world applications.

question.content: Design an assembly language program for the Intel 8051 microcontroller that interfaces with a temperature sensor through an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) and an LCD display. The program should read the temperature value, compare it with a threshold of $30^\circ\mathrm{C}$, and display "Hot" on the LCD if the temperature exceeds the threshold, or "Cold" if it is below the threshold.

answer.content:
### **Design of an 8051 Assembly Program for Temperature Monitoring System**

---

#### **1. System Architecture and Hardware Assumptions**

We consider an 8051 microcontroller interfaced with:
- **Temperature Sensor**: Outputs an analog voltage proportional to temperature (e.g., LM35 sensor: 10 mV/°C).
- **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**: 8-bit ADC0804 (or similar) connected via port pins.
- **LCD Display**: Standard 16×2 character LCD in 8-bit mode.

**Hardware Connections**:
- **ADC Interface**:
  - ADC data lines (D0–D7) → Port P1 of 8051.
  - Start-of-conversion (SOC) pin → P3.0 (output from 8051).
  - End-of-conversion (EOC) pin → P3.1 (input to 8051).
  - Output enable (OE) pin → P3.2 (output from 8051).
- **LCD Interface**:
  - Data lines (DB0–DB7) → Port P2.
  - Control pins:
    - RS (Register Select) → P3.4.
    - RW (Read/Write) → P3.5.
    - E (Enable) → P3.6.
> **Visual Representation: System Block Diagram**

#### **2. Program Design Strategy**

The program flowchart:
1. **Initialize** the LCD.
2. **Start ADC conversion** and read digital temperature value.
3. **Convert** raw ADC value to Celsius.
4. **Compare** with threshold (30°C).
5. **Display** "Hot" or "Cold" on LCD.
6. **Repeat** continuously.

**Key Calculations**:
- Assume ADC reference voltage $V_{\text{ref}} = 5\,\text{V}$.
- For 8-bit ADC: step size $\Delta V = \frac{5}{255} \approx 19.6\,\text{mV}$.
- LM35 sensor: $V_{\text{out}} = 10\,\text{mV/°C}$.
- Temperature in °C: $T = \frac{\text{ADC\_value} \times 19.6}{10} = \text{ADC\_value} \times 1.96$.
- Threshold in ADC units: $\frac{30}{1.96} \approx 15.3 \rightarrow$ compare with 15 (since ADC output is integer).

---
#### **3. Assembly Program Code**

;====================================================================
; 8051 Temperature Monitoring System
; Reads temperature from ADC, compares with 30°C threshold,
; and displays "Hot" or "Cold" on LCD.
;====================================================================

;-------- Port Definitions --------
ADC_DATA   EQU P1      ; ADC data lines
SOC        EQU P3.0    ; Start conversion pin
EOC        EQU P3.1    ; End-of-conversion pin
OE         EQU P3.2    ; Output enable pin

LCD_DATA   EQU P2      ; LCD data port
RS         EQU P3.4    ; Register select
RW         EQU P3.5    ; Read/write
EN         EQU P3.6    ; Enable

;-------- Constants --------
THRESHOLD  EQU 15      ; ADC value for 30°C (approx)

;-------- Register Usage --------
; R0: Temporary for loops
; R1: Stores ADC value
; R2: String pointer

;====================================================================
; MAIN PROGRAM
;====================================================================
ORG 0000H
    LJMP MAIN

ORG 0030H
MAIN:
    ACALL LCD_INIT      ; Initialize LCD
    ACALL DELAY_MS      ; Wait for LCD to settle

MAIN_LOOP:
    ACALL READ_ADC      ; Read temperature -> R1
    ACALL CHECK_TEMP    ; Compare with threshold
    ACALL DISPLAY_MSG   ; Display result
    SJMP MAIN_LOOP      ; Repeat forever

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: READ_ADC
; Reads 8-bit value from ADC0804 and returns in R1.
;====================================================================
READ_ADC:
    CLR SOC             ; Set SOC low
    SETB SOC            ; Pulse SOC high
    CLR SOC             ; Start conversion
WAIT_EOC:
    JB EOC, WAIT_EOC    ; Wait for EOC low
CONV_DONE:
    JNB EOC, CONV_DONE  ; Wait for EOC high (conversion done)
    SETB OE             ; Enable ADC output
    MOV R1, ADC_DATA    ; Read ADC value
    CLR OE              ; Disable ADC output
    RET

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: CHECK_TEMP
; Compares ADC value in R1 with THRESHOLD.
; Sets carry flag: C=1 if temperature > 30°C (Hot), else C=0 (Cold).
;====================================================================
CHECK_TEMP:
    MOV A, R1
    CLR C
    SUBB A, #THRESHOLD  ; A = ADC_value - 15
    ; After SUBB: if ADC_value >= 15 -> C=0 (no borrow), else C=1 (borrow)
    CPL C               ; Invert: C=1 for Hot, C=0 for Cold
    RET

;====================================================================
; SUBROUTINE: DISPLAY_MSG
; Displays "Hot" or "Cold" on LCD based on carry flag.
;====================================================================
DISPLAY_MSG:
    ACALL LCD_CLEAR     ; Clear display first
    JC DISPLAY_HOT      ; Jump if C=1 (Hot)

DISPLAY_COLD:
    MOV DPTR, #COLD_MSG ; Load cold message address
    SJMP PRINT_STRING

DISPLAY_HOT:
    MOV DPTR, #HOT_MSG  ; Load hot message address

PRINT_STRING:
    ACALL LCD_STRING    ; Display the string
    RET

;====================================================================
; LCD CONTROL SUBROUTINES
;====================================================================
LCD_INIT:
    MOV A, #38H         ; 8-bit mode, 2 lines, 5x7 font
    ACALL LCD_CMD
    MOV A, #0CH         ; Display ON, cursor OFF
    ACALL LCD_CMD
    MOV A, #06H         ; Entry mode: increment cursor
    ACALL LCD_CMD
    MOV A, #01H         ; Clear display
    ACALL LCD_CMD
    RET

LCD_CMD:                ; Send command in A to LCD
    CLR RS              ; RS=0 for command
    CLR RW              ; RW=0 for write
    MOV LCD_DATA, A
    SETB EN             ; Pulse enable
    CLR EN
    ACALL DELAY_MS
    RET

LCD_DATA_WRITE:         ; Send data in A to LCD
    SETB RS             ; RS=1 for data
    CLR RW
    MOV LCD_DATA, A
    SETB EN
    CLR EN
    ACALL DELAY_MS
    RET

LCD_STRING:             ; Display string pointed by DPTR
    PUSH ACC
NEXT_CHAR:
    CLR A
    MOVC A, @A+DPTR     ; Get character from code memory
    JZ STRING_END       ; End if null
    ACALL LCD_DATA_WRITE
    INC DPTR
    SJMP NEXT_CHAR
STRING_END:
    POP ACC
    RET

LCD_CLEAR:
    MOV A, #01H         ; Clear display command
    ACALL LCD_CMD
    RET

;====================================================================
; DELAY SUBROUTINES
;====================================================================
DELAY_MS:               ; Approx 1 ms delay at 12 MHz
    MOV R0, #250
DELAY_LOOP:
    NOP
    NOP
    DJNZ R0, DELAY_LOOP
    RET

;====================================================================
; DATA STORAGE
;====================================================================
ORG 0200H
HOT_MSG:    DB "Hot", 0
COLD_MSG:   DB "Cold", 0

END

---

#### **4. Detailed Explanation of Critical Sections**

##### **4.1 ADC Interfacing**
- The sequence for ADC0804:
  1. Pulse `SOC` high to start conversion.
  2. Wait for `EOC` to go low, then high (conversion complete).
  3. Set `OE` high to enable output.
  4. Read data from `ADC_DATA` (Port P1).
- Conversion time: ~100 µs, accommodated by software polling.

##### **4.2 Temperature Threshold Comparison**
- The raw ADC value $N$ is compared with threshold $T_{\text{adc}}$:
  $$T_{\text{adc}} = \frac{30}{1.96} \approx 15$$
- If $N \geq 15$, temperature > 30°C → "Hot".
- The `SUBB` instruction sets carry flag when $N < 15$. We invert the flag for easier branching.
T_{\text{adc}} = \frac{30}{1.96} \approx 15

##### **4.3 LCD String Display**
- Strings are stored in code memory with null termination.
- `LCD_STRING` uses `MOVC` to fetch characters and displays them until null (0) is encountered.

##### **4.4 Timing Considerations**
- **LCD Commands**: Require ~40 µs execution time; our `DELAY_MS` provides sufficient margin.
- **ADC Conversion**: Polling `EOC` ensures reliable synchronization.
- **Refresh Rate**: The main loop repeats continuously; typical update rate ~10 Hz.

1. **Accuracy**: The threshold comparison uses integer arithmetic. For better precision, use:
   - Lookup table for temperature conversion.
   - 16-bit arithmetic for exact comparison: $N \times 196 > 3000$ (since $T = N \times 196 / 100$).

2. **Efficiency**: 
   - Instead of clearing LCD every cycle, update only when message changes.
   - Use interrupt-driven ADC reading (if EOC connected to INT pin).

3. **Scalability**: 
   - Extendable for multiple thresholds by adding more comparisons.
